 
 
509 E Radburn Street, Tucson, AZ 85704 
www.economicintegrity.org 
Phone (520) 250-4416 
 
 
August 21, 2023 
To: 
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 
1700 G Street, NW 
Washington, DC 20552 
 
Re: 
Docket No. CFPB–2023–0038  
Response to CFPB request for information on medical debt 
 
In November 2022, Arizona voters, by an overwhelming 72 percent to 28 percent, 
approved a new law that restricts the interest on medical debt to no more than three 
percent1,2.  This was the first statewide ballot measure in the nation to restrict interest rates on 
medical debt, and it was widely seen as a model for other states. However, almost immediately, 
a huge loophole has come into focus. 
The Center for Economic Integrity became aware of the loophole after a volunteer 
noticed a card on the desk of a receptionist at Banner Health3 promoting a health-care line of 
credit of up to $10,000 offered by a company named Curae4. The volunteer wondered how 
Curae could make a profit on a three-percent loan. However, his visit to Curae’s website 
revealed the company wasn’t charging three percent, but annual interest rates of 9.9 percent 
to 36 percent5. 
Additionally, should a consumer make a late payment, it triggers an interest rate of 
29.99 percent that is accrued from the first day the money was borrowed. This penalty rate is 
charged until at least six consecutive minimum payments are made when due. This penalty 
applies even to no-interest loans if they are not paid off in time. 
                                                      
1 “Arizona Proposition 209, Healthcare Debt Interest Rate Limit and Debt Collection Exemptions Initiative (2022)” 
https://ballotpedia.org/Arizona_Proposition_209,_Healthcare_Debt_Interest_Rate_Limit_and_Debt_Collection_Exemptions_Initiative_(2022)  
2 “Proposition 209 Information Sheet”, at AZCourts.gov,  
https://www.azcourts.gov/selfservicecenter/Garnishment/Proposition-209  
3 Banner Health is an $8.7 trillion revenue health-care nonprofit headquartered in Phoenix, Arizona. 
https://www.bannerhealth.com/  
4 Curae is a subsidiary of Atlanticus Holdings (ATLC), a public company traded on Nasdaq and headquartered in 
Atlanta, Georgia.   
5 “Pricing Information Addendum” and “Cardholder Agreement”, accessed 08/18/2023. 
http://curae.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/Curae-Cardholder-Agreement-and-Pricing-Addendum.pdf  

 
2 
The loophole is that once the consumer takes out the loan to pay his or her medical bill, 
the debt is no longer medical debt. It is now credit debt, and the three percent interest rate no 
longer applies.  
HealthCare Rising Arizona, the primary sponsor of the voter initiative, won the first 
round of litigation over the new law6.  Now the case is in the appeals process. However, in the 
meantime state officials have made no effort to apply the three percent restriction to such 
loans taken out to pay medical expenses.  
The Center for Economic Integrity believes that such loans violate the spirit of the new 
Arizona law, which was passed to ensure no Arizona family should lose their home or car due to 
medical emergencies or accidents or be trapped into unending debt by unfair interest rates on 
medical care.  
But the provisions of Curae’s loan does exactly that – it traps borrowers. Its low or zero-
percent initial interest rate appeals to patients, but its penalty rate unfairly traps borrowers 
unfortunate enough to make a late payment into high interest rate debt that dramatically 
increases the cost of the patient’s health care. 
Arizona’s experience should be a cautionary tale to any other state that seeks to limit 
the interest rate on medical debt. It needs to make sure that debt incurred for medical care, 
even with credit cards or loans, is covered by its law restricting the interest on medical debt. 
Otherwise, their new law will be virtually meaningless. 
 
 
 
Kelly S, Griffith 
Executive Director,  
Center for Economic Integrity 
                                                      
6 https://www.azcentral.com/story/news/politics/arizona/2022/12/22/arizona-proposition-209-is-not-
unconstitutionally-vague-judge-rules/69735224007/
